name.text: Week 11 – Q2: Motional EMF — Sliding Bar on Rails

questiontext.text:
Motional EMF — Sliding Bar on Rails

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 5 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 0%

A conducting bar of length \(\ell = {@ell_cm@}\;\text{cm}\) slides along two parallel conducting rails at a constant velocity \(v = {@v@}\;\text{m/s}\) to the right. A uniform magnetic field \(\mathbf{B} = {@B@}\;\text{T}\) points into the page. The rails are connected at the left end by a resistor \(R = {@R@}\;\Omega\).

Part (a) — Motional EMF (1 point)

Calculate the magnitude of the motional EMF induced in the sliding bar.

\(\varepsilon = \) [[input:ans1]] V

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 1.20

[[validation:ans1]]

Part (b) — Current (1 point)

Calculate the magnitude of the current flowing through the circuit.

\(I = \) [[input:ans2]] A

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.200

[[validation:ans2]]

Part (c) — Power Dissipated (1 point)

Calculate the electrical power dissipated in the resistor.

\(P = \) [[input:ans3]] W

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 0.240

[[validation:ans3]]

Part (d) — Current Direction (1 point)

Using Lenz’s law, determine the direction of the induced current through the resistor.

[[input:ans4]]

[[validation:ans4]]

Part (e) — Parameter Sensitivity (1 point)

Suppose the bar length is doubled (\(\ell \to 2\ell\)) while the velocity is halved (\(v \to v/2\)). All other parameters remain unchanged. What happens to the power dissipated in the resistor?

[[input:ans5]]

[[validation:ans5]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: Motional EMF

As the bar moves to the right with velocity \(v\), it sweeps out new area at a rate \(\frac{dA}{dt} = \ell \cdot v\). The flux through the circuit increases as: \(\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = B \cdot \ell \cdot v\).

By Faraday’s law:

\[|\varepsilon| = B \ell v = {@B@} \times {@ell@} \times {@v@} = {@ta1@}\;\text{V}\]

Step 2: Current

By Ohm’s law:

\[I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = \frac{{@ta1@}}{{@R@}} = {@ta2@}\;\text{A}\]

Step 3: Power Dissipated

The electrical power dissipated in the resistor equals the mechanical power required to push the bar:

\[P = I^2 R = ({@ta2@})^2 \times {@R@} = {@ta3@}\;\text{W}\]

(Equivalently: \(P = \varepsilon \cdot I = {@ta1@} \times {@ta2@} = {@ta3@}\;\text{W}\))

Part (d): Current Direction

The bar moves right, increasing the enclosed area and thus the flux into the page. By Lenz’s law, the induced current must create a field that opposes this increase, so it creates a field out of the page inside the loop. By the right-hand rule, the current flows counterclockwise: upward through the bar and downward (from top to bottom) through the external resistor.

Part (e): Parameter Sensitivity

The power dissipated is \(P = I^2 R = \frac{(B \ell v)^2}{R}\). The product \(B \ell v\) determines the EMF.

If \(\ell \to 2\ell\) and \(v \to v/2\):

\[\varepsilon' = B \cdot (2\ell) \cdot (v/2) = B \ell v = \varepsilon\]

The EMF is unchanged, so the current and power are also unchanged. The doubling of \(\ell\) exactly compensates the halving of \(v\) because EMF depends on the product \(\ell v\).

Answer Summary

PartQuantityCorrect Answer
(a)\(\varepsilon\){@ta1@} V
(b)\(I\){@ta2@} A
(c)\(P\){@ta3@} W
(d)Current directionCounterclockwise (downward through R)
(e)Parameter changePower stays the same

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — Motional EMF

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Current

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — Power Dissipated

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — Current Direction

[[feedback:prt4]]

Part (e) — Parameter Sensitivity

[[feedback:prt5]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: ℓ={@ell_cm@}cm, v={@v@}m/s, B={@B@}T, R={@R@}Ω | ε={@ta1@}V, I={@ta2@}A, P={@ta3@}W

questiondescription.text: Motional EMF — Sliding bar on rails with resistor. Calculate EMF, current, power, and determine current direction via Lenz's law. Week 11 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: Close! Check your arithmetic. The answer is {@ta1@} V.
Your answer is off by about a factor of 10. Did you forget to convert \(\ell\) from cm to m?

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The motional EMF is \(\varepsilon = B\ell v = {@B@} \times {@ell@} \times {@v@} = {@ta1@}\;\text{V}\). Remember to convert \(\ell\) from cm to m.

truefeedback.text: Close! Check your arithmetic. The answer is {@ta2@} A.
Your answer is off by about a factor of 10. Check your EMF value or unit conversions.
Close! Check your arithmetic. The answer is {@ta3@} W.

node.description: Check I*R instead of I^2*R common error

truefeedback.text: It looks like you computed \(I \times R\) (which gives voltage, not power). Power is \(P = I^2 R\) or equivalently \(P = \varepsilon \times I\).
Your answer is off by about a factor of 10. Check your unit conversions.

Correct! The bar moves right, increasing flux into the page. Lenz's law requires the induced current to oppose this increase by creating flux out of the page inside the loop, so the current flows counterclockwise (downward through the resistor).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The bar moves right, increasing the enclosed area and thus the flux into the page. By Lenz's law, the induced current must oppose this increase: the induced field must point out of the page inside the loop. By the right-hand rule, the current flows counterclockwise (downward through the resistor).

truefeedback.text: Correct! Since \(P = (B\ell v)^2/R\) and the product \(\ell v\) is unchanged when \(\ell\) doubles and \(v\) halves, the power stays the same.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The key insight is that \(P = (B\ell v)^2/R\). The EMF depends on the product \(\ell v\). Doubling \(\ell\) and halving \(v\) gives \(2\ell \times v/2 = \ell v\), which is unchanged. Therefore the power stays the same.

hint.text: Hint 1 (Intuition): When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, the free charges inside it experience a force (Lorentz force). This creates an EMF. The faster the conductor moves or the stronger the field, the larger the EMF.

Hint 2 (Formulas): The motional EMF is \(\varepsilon = B\ell v\), where \(\ell\) is the length of the bar (convert cm to m!), \(v\) is the velocity, and \(B\) is the magnetic field strength. The current is \(I = \varepsilon/R\) and power is \(P = I^2 R\). For Lenz's law: determine how the flux changes as the bar moves, then find the current direction that opposes that change.

Hint 3 (Worked Step): Convert: \(\ell = {@ell_cm@}\;\text{cm} = {@ell@}\;\text{m}\). Then \(\varepsilon = {@B@} \times {@ell@} \times {@v@}\). Once you have \(\varepsilon\), divide by \(R = {@R@}\;\Omega\) to get \(I\), and compute \(P = I^2 \times R\). For the direction: the bar moves right, so the area of the loop increases. Determine how the flux through the loop changes, then apply Lenz's law — the induced current must flow in the direction that opposes that change in flux.
